children.name: Pteropus
children.description: Flying foxes — the largest bats on Earth, with wingspans reaching 1.7 metres. Unlike most bats, flying foxes navigate primarily by eyesight rather than echolocation (most have large, fox-like eyes). They roost in vast colonies called camps, sometimes numbering a million individuals, in trees visible for kilometres. Flying foxes are the primary pollinators and seed dispersers for hundreds of tropical tree species; the loss of flying fox populations leads to measurable declines in forest regeneration. Many species are threatened by hunting, habitat destruction, and persecution by fruit farmers.

children.name: Pteropus vampyrus
children.description: The largest bat in the world with a wingspan up to 1.7 m. Roosts in coastal mangroves and forest from Myanmar to Indonesia. Hunted in huge numbers for bushmeat; populations declining rapidly.

children.name: Pteropus giganteus
children.description: Sacred in many Hindu traditions; roosts in temple trees. Forms massive urban camps in Indian cities, sometimes with millions of bats. Forages up to 150 km from its roost in a single night.

children.name: Pteropus poliocephalus
children.description: Australia's largest bat and a vital pollinator of eucalyptus and native hardwoods. Listed as vulnerable; major camps have been disrupted by urban development and weather extremes.

children.name: Pteropus alecto
children.description: One of Australia's most abundant flying foxes, entirely jet-black except for a reddish-brown mantle. Wingspan reaches 1 m; roosts in riverside eucalyptus and mangroves from northern Australia to southern New Guinea. A key pollinator of Australian hardwoods; colonies shift location seasonally following flowering events.

children.name: Pteropus conspicillatus
children.description: Named for the distinctive pale straw-yellow spectacle ring around its large dark eyes. Restricted to tropical rainforest in north-east Queensland and New Guinea, where it is the dominant seed disperser for rainforest trees. Listed as endangered in Australia; mass die-off events linked to heat stress have killed tens of thousands in recent years.

children.name: Pteropus scapulatus
children.description: The smallest and most nomadic of the Australian flying foxes, with a wingspan of around 1 m and entirely reddish-brown fur. Forms the largest camps in Australia — sometimes exceeding 1 million animals — that follow eucalyptus and paperback blossom across the continent. Its nomadism and sheer abundance make it the most important bat pollinator in Australia.

children.name: Pteropus rufus
children.description: The only flying fox native to Madagascar, with rich chestnut-red fur and a wingspan reaching 1.2 m. Roosts colonially in large trees throughout the island's forests, coastal areas, and villages. Heavily hunted for bushmeat; listed as vulnerable with a rapidly declining population.

children.name: Pteropus hypomelanus
children.description: One of the most widespread flying foxes in Southeast Asia and Oceania, occurring across hundreds of island groups from India to Fiji. Shows considerable geographic variation in coat colour, giving rise to its common name. Roosts on small coral and continental islands, often in mangrove forests, and serves as a critical inter-island pollinator.

children.name: Pteropus lylei
children.description: A mid-sized flying fox restricted to Thailand, Cambodia, and Vietnam, roosting in large temple trees in urban areas. Populations have declined sharply due to hunting and roost disturbance. Plays a key role in pollinating durian and other commercially important fruit crops in mainland Southeast Asia.

children.name: Pteropus niger
children.description: Endemic to the islands of Mauritius and Réunion in the Indian Ocean. A large, dark-bodied bat with a wingspan over 1 m, playing an essential role as pollinator and seed disperser on these islands. Culled by the Mauritian government as an alleged crop pest, reducing the population by hundreds of thousands despite strong scientific opposition.

children.name: Pteropus rodricensis
children.description: One of the world's most endangered bats, restricted to the tiny island of Rodrigues in the Indian Ocean. Population dropped to fewer than 100 individuals after cyclone damage in the 1970s but has recovered to several thousand following intensive conservation action. Bright orange-red fur on the mantle; plays a central seed dispersal role in Rodrigues forest restoration.

children.name: Pteropus livingstonii
children.description: Critically endangered and restricted to the islands of Anjouan and Mohéli in the Comoros archipelago. One of the largest Old World fruit bats, entirely black with a wingspan over 1.4 m. Habitat loss through deforestation has reduced the population to fewer than 1,200 individuals; a captive breeding programme exists in British zoos.

children.name: Pteropus seychellensis
children.description: The only native bat of the Seychelles inner island granitic group, with an estimated population of 70,000–100,000. Feeds on fruit, nectar, and pollen across the archipelago; considered a crop pest by orchard owners and subject to periodic culling. Essential pollinator of native Seychellois trees that have no other vertebrate pollinators.

children.name: Pteropus tonganus
children.description: Distributed across islands of the western Pacific from the Solomon Islands east to Tonga and the Cook Islands. One of the larger Pacific flying foxes; an important food item in Tongan culture and still legally hunted in Tonga and Samoa. A key seed disperser and pollinator on Pacific islands with few other frugivorous vertebrates.

children.name: Pteropus samoensis
children.description: A large flying fox restricted to Samoa and Fiji, where it is regarded as a chiefly animal and featured in Samoan oral tradition. Unlike most flying foxes, Samoan flying foxes are frequently diurnal, foraging in daylight hours in the forest canopy. Severely impacted by hunting; protected in American Samoa but still hunted in Western Samoa.

children.name: Pteropus mariannus
children.description: Native to the Mariana Islands in the western Pacific; listed as endangered due to intensive commercial hunting and introduced snake predation. A traditional food of the Chamorro people and heavily targeted by hunters using spotlights; imported by the ton from elsewhere in the Pacific. Critical pollinator and seed disperser of Mariana forest trees.

children.name: Pteropus woodfordi
children.description: A medium-sized flying fox endemic to the Solomon Islands, where it roosts colonially in lowland forest. Characterised by dark brown to black fur with a paler mantle. Like most Solomon Island bats, it is hunted for food and is thought to be in decline due to habitat loss.

children.name: Pteropus temminckii
children.description: A medium-sized flying fox restricted to the Moluccas and surrounding islands of eastern Indonesia. Dark brown above with a pale yellowish mantle; roosts in small groups in forest trees. Poorly studied and considered vulnerable due to hunting pressure and limited range across small oceanic islands.

children.name: Pteropus leucopterus
children.description: A critically endangered flying fox endemic to Luzon in the Philippines, known for the striking whitish or pale-straw translucency of the wing membranes. Only a handful of known roosts survive; deforestation and hunting have pushed the species to the brink of extinction. Virtually nothing is known of its ecology.

children.name: Pteropus anetianus
children.description: Endemic to Vanuatu and nearby Santa Cruz Islands; a medium-sized, dark-furred flying fox with a warm reddish-brown collar. Forages across lowland forest and secondary growth; hunted for bushmeat and impacted by cyclone-related habitat disturbance. Population status poorly known.

children.name: Pteropus admiralitatum
children.description: Restricted to the Admiralty Islands and surrounding island groups of Papua New Guinea. A medium-sized flying fox with dark brown-black fur and a pale collar; roosts in lowland and hill forest. Hunted locally and subject to habitat loss from logging.

children.name: Pteropus alecto
children.description: The most common flying fox in northern Australia, with a black body and reddish-brown mantle. Forms large camps in mangroves and paperbark swamps. Known for its raucous daytime squabbling that can be heard from a considerable distance, and for flying long distances inland to feed on blossom and fruit.

children.name: Pteropus conspicillatus
children.description: A large flying fox of the Queensland wet tropics, named for the pale-yellow fur around its eyes giving a 'spectacled' appearance. Its population has declined catastrophically due to heat stress events, with an estimated 23,000 individuals dying in a single extreme heatwave in 2018. Listed as Endangered.

children.name: Pteropus scapulatus
children.description: The smallest Australian flying fox, with a reddish-brown body. It is the most nomadic of the flying foxes, following the flowering of eucalypts across the continent. Its camps can contain over a million individuals, making it one of the most numerous bat species in Australia.

children.name: Pteropus lombocensis
children.description: A small flying fox endemic to the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia. Its conservation status is poorly known but it is likely threatened by habitat loss and hunting.

children.name: Pteropus griseus
children.description: A small, greyish flying fox of Sulawesi, Timor, and adjacent islands. Roosts in small colonies in coastal forests and feeds on nectar and fruit.

children.name: Pteropus chrysou
children.description: A golden-brown flying fox endemic to the Philippine island of Palawan. Threatened by deforestation and hunting. Listed as Near Threatened.

children.name: Pteropus speciosus
children.description: A medium-sized flying fox of the southern Philippines and northern Borneo. Distinctive for its bright golden mantle contrasting with the dark body. It roosts in mixed-species colonies in coastal and lowland forests.

children.name: Pteropus hypoleucus
children.description: Found in the Moluccas and Sulawesi. Highly variable in colour, from almost black to pale creamy-brown. Like many island flying foxes, it is threatened by overhunting.

children.name: Pteropus ornata
children.description: A strikingly patterned flying fox from the Philippines, with a pale golden head and dark brown body. Listed as Endangered due to deforestation and hunting for the bushmeat trade.

children.name: Pteropus molossinus
children.description: A small flying fox endemic to the Caroline Islands in Micronesia. It is the only mammal native to the island of Pohnpei. Threatened by typhoons, deforestation, and hunting.

children.name: Pteropus tonganus
children.description: The most widespread Pacific flying fox, found from the Solomon Islands to the Cook Islands. It roosts in large colonies in coastal forests and is an important pollinator of many Pacific island trees. Known to fly up to 80 km between roosting and feeding sites in a single night.

children.name: Pteropus rainworthii
children.description: A flying fox of the Lesser Sunda Islands and Timor, named after the naturalist Rainworth. One of the lesser-known Pteropus species of the Indonesian archipelago.

children.name: Pteropus personatus
children.description: A small flying fox from the Moluccas with a distinctive dark facial mask contrasting with the pale fur of the head. Its small size and primitive skull features make it one of the more basal members of the Pteropus genus.

children.name: Pteropus mackayi
children.description: A species of flying fox known from just a few localities in the Solomon Islands. Named after the naturalist Roy Mackay. Little is known of its ecology but it is presumed to be threatened by the extensive deforestation affecting the Solomon Islands lowland forests.

children.name: Pteropus mariannus
children.description: A medium-sized flying fox endemic to the Mariana Islands. Its populations have been devastated by the introduction of the brown tree snake (Boiga irregularis) on Guam, which has caused the complete extirpation of the species from that island, and by overhunting on other islands where it is now critically endangered with fewer than 500 mature individuals estimated to survive across its fragmented range.

children.name: Pteropus pselaphon
children.description: A critically endangered flying fox endemic to the Bonin (Ogasawara) Islands of Japan. With a population of fewer than 200 individuals, it is one of the rarest bats in the world. Hunted to near-extinction for its fur, which was reportedly used in traditional Japanese archery equipment (as padding for arrow quivers and grip materials), and now threatened by introduced rats and feral cats.

children.name: Pteropus albiventer
children.description: A medium-sized flying fox of the Moluccas with a distinctive white belly contrasting with the dark brown back and wings.

children.name: Pteropus aldobrensis
children.description: Endemic to the Aldabra Atoll in the Seychelles. Its population is restricted to the small islands of the atoll.

children.name: Pteropus antisthenes
children.description: A flying fox endemic to the Mide Island group of the Philippines. Its small population is threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Pteropus ballae
children.description: A little-known flying fox of the Lesser Sunda Islands. Its taxonomy is uncertain.

children.name: Pteropus banakris
children.description: A recently described species from the Indonesian archipelago. Its name is derived from local terminology.

children.name: Pteropus chrysou
children.description: A striking golden-brown flying fox of the Philippines, with a distinctive yellow-gold mantle contrasting with the dark brown back.

children.name: Pteropus comunis
children.description: A widespread flying fox of the Moluccas and Sulawesi. Its common name reflects its abundance throughout its limited range.

children.name: Pteropus coxii
children.description: A flying fox of the Solomon Islands. Named after the naturalist Cox.

children.name: Pteropus dastoni
children.description: A recently described flying fox from the Mentawai Islands of Indonesia. Named after the mammalogist Daston.

children.name: Pteropus debbie
children.description: A flying fox of the D'Entrecasteaux Islands of Papua New Guinea. Little is known of its ecology.

children.name: Pteropus florensis
children.description: A flying fox endemic to the island of Flores, Indonesia. Its population is threatened by deforestation.

children.name: Pteropus fundatus
children.description: A flying fox of the Banks Islands of Vanuatu. Restricted to a few small islands.

children.name: Pteropus gibbsi
children.description: A recently described species from the Solomon Islands. Named after the mammalogist Gibbs.

children.name: Pteropus gilliardorum
children.description: A flying fox of the Bismarck Archipelago, named after the ornithologist E. Thomas Gilliard.

children.name: Pteropus grandis
children.description: A large flying fox of the Solomon Islands. Its large size and absence of a mantle distinguish it from sympatric Pteropus species.

children.name: Pteropus howensis
children.description: Endemic to Lord Howe Island, where it was once abundant before cats and rats reduced its population. One of the most endangered flying foxes.

children.name: Pteropus keyensis
children.description: A flying fox of the Kai Islands of Indonesia. Its small island range makes it vulnerable to habitat loss.

children.name: Pteropus leucogenys
children.description: A small flying fox of the Ryukyu Islands of Japan. Distinguished by white patches on the cheeks. It is the northernmost Pteropus species, ranging as far north as Yakushima Island south of Kyushu, where it is the only fruit bat species present and an important pollinator of the region's subtropical forests.

children.name: Pteropus livingstonii
children.description: A critically endangered giant flying fox endemic to the Comoros Islands. With a wingspan of up to 1.8 m, it is one of the largest bats in the world. Its population numbers fewer than 1,000 individuals, restricted to the remaining patches of native forest on the slopes of Mount Karthala on Grand Comore and Mount Mtingui on Anjouan. Named after the explorer David Livingstone by the curator of the Natural History Museum's mammal collections, who was inspired by Livingstone's accounts of the giant fruit bats of Africa — though Livingstone himself never reached the Comoros and probably never saw the species that bears his name.

children.name: Pteropus lombocensis
children.description: A small flying fox of the Lesser Sunda Islands, from Lombok to Timor. Its ability to cross narrow sea channels has allowed it to colonise several islands in this chain.

children.name: Pteropus macrotis
children.description: A medium-sized flying fox with notably large ears, found on New Guinea, the Aru Islands, and the northern tip of Queensland. Its large ears provide enhanced hearing for locating fruits in dense forest.

children.name: Pteropus mahaganus
children.description: A flying fox of the Sangir and Talaud Islands of Indonesia. Its small range between Sulawesi and Mindanao makes it vulnerable.

children.name: Pteropus malacensis
children.description: A flying fox of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo. Its taxonomy has been debated, with some authorities treating it as a subspecies of P. vampyrus.

children.name: Pteropus melanopogon
children.description: A large flying fox of the Moluccas with a distinctive black beard-like tuft of hair on the chin.

children.name: Pteropus molossinus
children.description: A small flying fox endemic to the Caroline Islands. It is the only native mammal on Pohnpei and is a key pollinator of the island's trees.

children.name: Pteropus murcianus
children.description: A flying fox of the Sunda Islands, named after the mammalogist Murgia.

children.name: Pteropus neophyllus
children.description: A small flying fox of the Moluccas. Its specific name means 'new leaf', perhaps referring to its diet of tender foliage.

children.name: Pteropus nigricans
children.description: A uniformly dark flying fox of the Philippines and Sulawesi. Its simple, unmarked dark plumage is unusual among Pteropus species.

children.name: Pteropus nitendiens
children.description: A flying fox of the Solomon Islands with a notably glossy, sheeny coat.

children.name: Pteropus obscurus
children.description: A dark brown flying fox of the Moluccas. Its sombre colouration gives it its name.

children.name: Pteropus pelagicus
children.description: A flying fox of the Caroline and Marshall Islands in Micronesia, capable of long-distance flights between islands.

children.name: Pteropus peronii
children.description: A flying fox of the Lesser Sunda Islands, named after the French naturalist François Péron.

children.name: Pteropus personatus
children.description: A small flying fox of the Moluccas with a distinctive dark facial mask. Its small size and primitive skull morphology suggest it is one of the most basal extant Pteropus.

children.name: Pteropus pilosus
children.description: A large flying fox of Palau in Micronesia. Its thick, woolly fur may be an adaptation to the high rainfall of its island home.

children.name: Pteropus pohlei
children.description: A flying fox of the Moluccas, named after the German mammalogist Hermann Pohle.

children.name: Pteropus poliopis
children.description: A flying fox of the Philippines with a greyish face, contrasting with its dark brown body.

children.name: Pteropus pumilus
children.description: A small flying fox of the Philippines and Sulawesi, with a striking golden mantle.

children.name: Pteropus raei
children.description: A flying fox of the Bismarck Archipelago, named after the naturalist Rae.

children.name: Pteropus rennellensis
children.description: Endemic to Rennell Island in the Solomon Islands. Its population is restricted to the island's pristine forests.

children.name: Pteropus rodricensis
children.description: A medium-sized flying fox endemic to Rodrigues Island in the Indian Ocean. Once one of the rarest bats in the world, its population has recovered from just 70–100 individuals in the 1970s to around 6,000 thanks to intensive conservation efforts.

children.name: Pteropus rufus
children.description: A large flying fox endemic to Madagascar. It is the largest bat on the island and one of the most important pollinators of the island's endemic trees.

children.name: Pteropus samoensis
children.description: A medium-sized flying fox of Samoa and Fiji. Unlike most flying foxes, it is active during the day, a behaviour that has made it vulnerable to hunting by humans.

children.name: Pteropus scapulatus
children.description: The smallest Australian flying fox, with a reddish-brown body. It is the most nomadic Australian fruit bat, following the flowering of eucalypts.

children.name: Pteropus seychellensis
children.description: A medium-sized flying fox found across the Seychelles and on the Mascarene Islands of Réunion and Mauritius. It is one of the most common large mammals of the Seychelles.

children.name: Pteropus subniger
children.description: A fruit bat of the Mascarene Islands, now extinct on Réunion but persisting on Mauritius. Its extinction on Réunion was caused by hunting and deforestation.

children.name: Pteropus temninkii
children.description: A flying fox of the Moluccas, named after the Dutch zoologist Coenraad Jacob Temminck.

children.name: Pteropus tokudale
children.description: A flying fox of the Ryukyu Islands and Taiwan. Its population has been severely reduced by habitat loss.

children.name: Pteropus tubbi
children.description: A flying fox of the Solomon Islands, named after the naturalist Tubb.

children.name: Pteropus uropos
children.description: A flying fox of the Philippines and Sulawesi with a distinctive marking on the tail membrane.

children.name: Pteropus vampyrus
children.description: One of the largest bats in the world by wingspan (up to 1.7 m). Found across Southeast Asia from Myanmar to the Philippines. Despite its name, it is a frugivore and does not drink blood. It forms enormous roosting colonies that can number tens of thousands of individuals. The species has suffered dramatic declines across its range due to hunting for bushmeat and traditional medicine.

children.name: Pteropus vetulus
children.description: A flying fox of the Moluccas and Timor. Its specific name means 'old' but the reason is obscure.

children.name: Pteropus voeltzkowii
children.description: Endemic to the island of Pemba off the coast of Tanzania. Its small island range and the continuing loss of its forest habitat have made it one of the most threatened Pteropus species in the western Indian Ocean.

children.name: Pteropus woodfordi
children.description: A flying fox of the Solomon Islands, named after the naturalist Charles M. Woodford.

children.name: Pteropus yapensis
children.description: Endemic to Yap in the Caroline Islands. It is the largest native mammal on the island and a major seed disperser for the island's forests.

children.name: Acerodon
children.description: Giant golden-crowned flying foxes — a small genus of large fruit bats from the Philippines and surrounding islands, closely related to Pteropus. Some authorities treat Acerodon as a subgenus of Pteropus. The golden-capped fruit bat is the largest bat by body mass, rivalling Pteropus vampyrus in wingspan. Acerodon species are severely threatened by deforestation and hunting; two species are critically endangered or possibly extinct.

children.name: Acerodon jubatus
children.description: The world's largest bat by body mass, weighing over 1.2 kg with a wingspan nearing 1.7 m. Endemic to the Philippines, it forms mega-camps of up to 1.8 million bats in Subic Bay and similar roost sites. Critically endangered; its roosting trees have been heavily logged and it is relentlessly hunted for bushmeat.

children.name: Acerodon celebensis
children.description: A large fruit bat restricted to Sulawesi and surrounding Indonesian islands. Dark brownish-black with a pale mantle; roosts in lowland and montane forest. Hunted for bushmeat and impacted by continued deforestation; considered vulnerable by the IUCN.

children.name: Acerodon mackloti
children.description: A medium-large fruit bat of the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia, including Flores, Sumbawa, and Timor. Dark brown to black with a paler mantle; behaviour and ecology poorly known. Considered vulnerable due to hunting and the small total area of its island range.

children.name: Eidolon
children.description: Straw-coloured fruit bats — a small genus of two large, highly migratory African fruit bats. Eidolon helvum produces the single largest mammal migration on Earth, when up to 10 million individuals converge on Kasanka National Park in Zambia each November. These bats are critical seed dispersers for African savanna and forest trees over continental scales. Both species are hunted for bushmeat, with Eidolon helvum being one of the most commercially harvested bats in Africa.

children.name: Eidolon helvum
children.description: The most abundant bat in Africa; a single migratory aggregation at Kasanka, Zambia, holds up to 10 million individuals — the world's largest mammal migration. Feeds on figs, oil palms, and nectar across sub-Saharan Africa and the Arabian Peninsula. Harvested by the million for bushmeat in West and Central Africa.

children.name: Eidolon dupreanum
children.description: Endemic to Madagascar; slightly smaller than its African congener with a more reddish tint to its straw-yellow fur. Roosts in caves and cliff faces in western and northern Madagascar; critical seed disperser for baobab and other dry-forest trees. Listed as vulnerable; hunted for bushmeat and impacted by cave disturbance.

children.name: Rousettus
children.description: Rousette fruit bats — medium-sized fruit bats of Africa and Asia, unique among the Pteropodidae in using a primitive form of echolocation produced by tongue clicks (not laryngeal like microchiroptera). The Egyptian fruit bat is the only Old World fruit bat with a well-studied echolocation system. Rousette bats often roost in caves in enormous numbers, and their caves are among the most spectacular wildlife spectacles on Earth — some colonies exceed a million bats. They are confirmed natural hosts for Marburg virus.

children.name: Rousettus aegyptiacus
children.description: The most widespread fruit bat in Africa. Roosts in caves and ruins; the Kasanka roost in Zambia holds several million individuals. Confirmed reservoir for Marburg virus, a haemorrhagic fever.

children.name: Rousettus amplexicaudatus
children.description: The most abundant cave-roosting fruit bat in Southeast Asia, with individual caves holding up to 1.8 million bats. Distributed from Myanmar to the Philippines and Timor; roosts in darkness zones of limestone caves. A confirmed host for Nipah-related henipaviruses; its guano is harvested commercially as fertiliser across its range.

children.name: Rousettus leschenaultii
children.description: A medium-sized rousette distributed from Pakistan through India, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia to southern China. Roosts in caves, ruins, and occasionally large hollow trees; one of the most frequently studied fruit bats in South Asia. Feeds heavily on figs, bananas, and mango; important pollinator of wild mango.

children.name: Rousettus madagascariensis
children.description: One of only two native bats of Madagascar that roost in caves, forming colonies of up to 100,000 in limestone cave systems. Feeds on forest fruit and nectar across much of Madagascar's forested regions. Vulnerable to disturbance of its limited number of critical roost caves.

children.name: Rousettus spinalatus
children.description: A distinctive rousette from Borneo and Sumatra characterised by partially naked skin visible along the midline of the back, where the wing membranes attach. Roosts in large cave colonies; closely associated with limestone karst landscapes. Poorly known; the naked-back patch may serve a thermoregulatory function.

children.name: Rousettus aegyptiacus
children.description: The most widespread Rousettus, ranging from Turkey and Cyprus through the Middle East and across sub-Saharan Africa. It roosts in caves and dark old buildings in colonies of up to several thousand. Its echolocation clicks are audible to humans as a series of sharp taps. Important pollinators of baobab and other African trees.

children.name: Rousettus amplexicaudatus
children.description: A widespread species of Southeast Asia, from Myanmar to the Solomon Islands. Roosts in caves in colonies of hundreds to many thousands. Its echolocation clicks are among the loudest produced by any Rousettus.

children.name: Rousettus leschenaultii
children.description: Found from Pakistan to Java. Its roost in the famous Bat Caves of the Batu Caves temple complex in Malaysia is a major tourist attraction. An important seed disperser for tropical fruit trees.

children.name: Rousettus madagascariensis
children.description: Endemic to Madagascar, where it roosts in caves and tree hollows in the eastern rainforest. The only Rousettus on the island. Listed as Vulnerable due to hunting and cave disturbance.

children.name: Cynopterus
children.description: Short-nosed fruit bats of South and Southeast Asia — small, camp-forming bats that roost in the foliage of palms and banyan trees rather than caves. They tent-build by chewing through leaf veins, causing the leaf to fold into a shelter. Cynopterus bats are among the most important pollinators of durian and other commercially valuable tropical fruits in Southeast Asia. Their decline through hunting and habitat loss has measurable economic consequences for tropical fruit agriculture.

children.name: Cynopterus sphinx
children.description: The most common fruit bat across South and Southeast Asia. A critical pollinator of durian, banana, and kapok. Males construct and maintain leaf tents to shelter harems.

children.name: Cynopterus brachyotis
children.description: One of the most widely distributed small fruit bats in Southeast Asia, ranging from southern India and Sri Lanka through the Malay Peninsula to the Philippines and Sulawesi. Tolerates disturbed and urban habitats well; a frequent visitor to gardens and fruit trees in cities. Forms small harem groups roosting in folded palm and banana leaves.

children.name: Cynopterus horsfieldi
children.description: A robust fruit bat of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo, and the Philippines, slightly larger than other Cynopterus species. Feeds on soft fruits and nectar in primary and secondary lowland forest. Males maintain and defend leaf tents sheltering one or more females; a model species for the study of bat tent-building behaviour.

children.name: Macroglossus
children.description: Long-tongued nectar bats — tiny, highly specialised fruit bats of Southeast Asia and Australia with an extremely elongated tongue and reduced dentition adapted for nectar feeding. They are the smallest members of the family Pteropodidae, weighing as little as 13 g. Macroglossus bats are the principal pollinators of the commercially important durian fruit and of many wild trees including balsa relatives; they hover at flowers like hummingbirds. Their small size, rapid reproduction, and nocturnal habits make them difficult to study.

children.name: Macroglossus minimus
children.description: The smallest pteropodid bat; weighs 13–27 g and roosts singly in rolled banana or heliconia leaves. Ranges from Myanmar to northern Australia; the most widespread member of the genus. The primary pollinator of banana flowers across its range and an important nectar bat for rainforest trees.

children.name: Macroglossus sobrinus
children.description: Slightly larger than its congener M. minimus; distributed from Nepal and northeastern India through Southeast Asia to the Philippines. Feeds almost exclusively on nectar and pollen; a key pollinator of durian in Borneo and Sumatra. Roosts in small groups in furled banana leaves in lowland forest.

children.name: Nyctimene
children.description: Tube-nosed fruit bats — a distinctive genus of small to medium fruit bats of New Guinea and Australasia, immediately recognised by the tubular, laterally projecting nostrils for which they are named. The function of the tubular nostrils is debated; hypotheses include enhanced olfactory discrimination of ripe fruit and prevention of juice inhalation while feeding. Nyctimene bats are mostly solitary forest-dwellers; their spotted wing membranes provide superb camouflage against lichen-covered bark.

children.name: Nyctimene albiventer
children.description: The most widespread tube-nosed fruit bat, distributed across the Moluccas, New Guinea, and northern Australia. Roosts singly in forest foliage, relying on cryptic spotted markings for camouflage. Feeds on a wide variety of soft fruits and figs; an important seed disperser in island rainforests.

children.name: Nyctimene cephalotes
children.description: A medium-sized tube-nosed bat from the Sulawesi and Philippine island groups. Distinguished by a relatively large, broad head and robust build for the genus. Roosts alone in secondary forest and forest edge; poorly studied, but foraging ecology typical of other Nyctimene.

children.name: Nyctimene robinsoni
children.description: The only tube-nosed bat native to Australia, restricted to the tropical rainforests of north-east Queensland. Forages nocturnally on rainforest fruits, often returning to the same forest stump or log to eat. A near-threatened species due to its restricted range and sensitivity to rainforest fragmentation.

children.name: Nyctimene aello
children.description: One of the larger tube-nosed bats; distributed across lowland and hill forests of New Guinea and the D'Entrecasteaux Islands. Solitary and largely sedentary; forages in the middle storeys of primary rainforest. The species is named for the Harpy of Greek mythology.

children.name: Nyctimene major
children.description: Distributed across the Solomon Islands and Bismarck Archipelago; the largest tube-nosed bat. Roosts singly under large rainforest leaves, relying on its spotted wings for daytime concealment. Poorly known; vulnerable to logging-driven forest loss across its island range.

children.name: Syconycteris
children.description: Blossom bats — a small genus of tiny, brush-tongued nectar bats from New Guinea and Australia. Related to Macroglossus but more compact; weighing 15–25 g. Syconycteris bats are the primary pollinators of many Australian and New Guinean heath plants, banksias, and grevilleas. The common blossom bat is an important pollinator of eucalyptus and melaleuca in tropical Australia; its tongue is equipped with papillae that trap pollen and nectar efficiently.

children.name: Syconycteris australis
children.description: A tiny nectar bat of coastal eastern Australia and New Guinea; one of the smallest pteropodids at 15–18 g. Primary pollinator of banksias, grevilleas, and eucalyptus in east Australian coastal forests. Highly nomadic; follows flowering events over hundreds of kilometres.

children.name: Syconycteris hobbit
children.description: A tiny blossom bat restricted to the moss forests of the central highlands of New Guinea at elevations above 1,500 m. Described in 1983, its colloquial name references J.R.R. Tolkien's fictional creature due to its diminutive stature. Feeds on nectar of montane forest flowers; little studied beyond its description.

children.name: Epomophorus
children.description: Epauletted fruit bats — a genus of medium-sized African fruit bats named for the shoulder pouches, or epaulettes, present in males. When displaying, males erect white hairs from these pouches and emit a rhythmic, melodious call audible for tens of metres — one of the most evocative sounds of the African night. Epauletted bats are important pollinators and seed dispersers in African savanna woodlands; the genus is widespread across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Epomophorus wahlbergi
children.description: The most common and widespread epauletted fruit bat in eastern and southern Africa, occurring in woodland and savanna from Sudan to South Africa. Males produce a distinctive double-note 'ping' call from prominent roost sites to attract females during the breeding season. An important pollinator of baobab and marula trees.

children.name: Epomophorus gambianus
children.description: Widespread across the woodlands and forest-savanna mosaic of West and Central Africa. Roosts in small groups in tree foliage; males call persistently from leks during breeding seasons. Consumed as bushmeat across much of its range and regarded as a crop pest in mango and guava orchards.

children.name: Epomophorus labiatus
children.description: Distributed across the Ethiopian highlands and East African savanna to Tanzania and Mozambique. Characterised by notably large, broad lips — reflected in the species name — and a comparatively robust skull. Forages on figs, wild fruits, and seasonal flowers; adapts readily to cultivated landscapes.

children.name: Epomophorus wahlbergi
children.description: A widespread species of eastern and southern Africa. Named after the Swedish naturalist Johan August Wahlberg. Common in urban parks and gardens.

children.name: Epomophorus gambianus
children.description: Found from Senegal to Nigeria. Its shoulder tufts are strikingly white and large in males. A common visitor to fruit plantations.

children.name: Epomophorus anurus
children.description: A smaller epauletted fruit bat of the African savanna, from Angola to Tanzania. Its high-pitched honking calls are distinct from the deeper calls of larger congeners.

children.name: Epomophorus grandis
children.description: One of the largest Epomophorus, found in the rainforests of the Congo Basin. Its shoulder tufts are exceptionally large and prominent.

children.name: Hypsignathus
children.description: Hammer-headed bat — a monotypic genus containing the largest bat in continental Africa. Males have a grotesquely enlarged hammer-shaped head — an adaptation for producing the extraordinarily loud honking calls used to attract females at leks. The lek mating system of Hypsignathus monstrosus is one of very few known in bats. The species is a confirmed wildlife reservoir for Ebola virus and Marburg virus. It is hunted heavily for bushmeat across Central and West Africa.

children.name: Hypsignathus monstrosus
children.description: The largest bat in continental Africa, with a wingspan up to 97 cm. Males have an extraordinarily enlarged larynx filling most of the chest cavity, producing resonant honking calls at leks where females choose mates. A confirmed reservoir for Ebola and Marburg viruses; hunted in large numbers for bushmeat across the Congo Basin.

children.name: Epomops
children.description: Singing fruit bats — a small genus of medium-sized African fruit bats closely related to Epomophorus. Males similarly possess shoulder pouches from which they erect white hair tufts, and they sing from prominent perches. However, Epomops males also sing extended, multi-phrase songs from concealed positions, a behaviour less typical of their Epomophorus relatives. They are forest-dependent bats of West and Central Africa; at least one species (Epomops franqueti) is a confirmed Ebola virus reservoir.

children.name: Epomops franqueti
children.description: A large, forest-dependent fruit bat of Central and West Africa; a confirmed wildlife reservoir for Ebola virus. Males exhibit a distinctive loud singing behaviour from concealed perches. Feeds on figs, soft fruits, and nectar; an important seed disperser in the Congo Basin rainforest.

children.name: Epomops dobsonii
children.description: A medium-sized epauletted fruit bat of montane and lowland forest in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, Rwanda, and Burundi. Poorly known; considered near-threatened due to its restricted montane forest habitat. Males sing from low perches in the forest understorey.

children.name: Epomops buettikoferi
children.description: A poorly known fruit bat of West African forests from Guinea to Ivory Coast. Characterised by a yellowish tinge to the fur and relatively large epaulettes. Ecology largely unstudied; likely plays a seed dispersal role in West African tropical forest similar to other Epomops species.

children.name: Megaloglossus
children.description: Woermann's bat — a monotypic genus containing the only strictly nectarivorous bat in Africa. Unlike the opportunistic nectar-feeding of epauletted bats, Megaloglossus woermanni feeds almost exclusively on the nectar of specific forest flowers, particularly those of the African oil palm. Its elongated muzzle, reduced teeth, and brush-tipped tongue are all specialisations for a nectar diet. It is the primary pollinator of the economically important African oil palm in its natural forest range.

children.name: Megaloglossus woermanni
children.description: Africa's only obligate nectar bat; feeds almost exclusively on the nectar of African oil palm and other forest flowers using its elongated brush-tipped tongue. Distributed across lowland forest from Guinea to Uganda. The primary natural pollinator of the African oil palm in wild forest settings.

children.name: Lissonycteris
children.description: Angolan fruit bat — a monotypic genus of medium-sized African fruit bats closely related to Myonycteris and formerly included within it. Lissonycteris angolensis inhabits montane and lowland forest across a broad band of Central and East Africa. It is characterised by smooth, short fur and a plain face lacking the distinctive collar markings of its Myonycteris relatives. Ecology poorly known; a likely seed disperser and pollinator in Afromontane forest.

children.name: Lissonycteris angolensis
children.description: A medium-sized, plain brown fruit bat of forest and forest-edge habitats from Sierra Leone to Ethiopia and south to Angola. Feeds on a wide variety of forest fruits; roosts in small groups in dense foliage. Least concern; tolerates moderately disturbed habitats.

children.name: Myonycteris
children.description: Little collared fruit bats — a small genus of small, round-eared African forest fruit bats, named for the pale collar of creamy or orange fur on the neck that distinguishes most species. They are among the smallest members of the African Pteropodidae, weighing only 30–55 g. Myonycteris bats roost in small groups in dense forest foliage; their ecology is poorly known but they likely serve as important pollinators and seed dispersers in African rainforest and montane forest.

children.name: Myonycteris torquata
children.description: The most widespread of the little collared fruit bats, occurring across the lowland forests of West and Central Africa from Gambia to the DRC. Characterised by a pale orange-buff collar contrasting with darker brown body fur. Feeds on figs and soft forest fruit; a confirmed Ebola virus seropositive species.

children.name: Myonycteris relicta
children.commonName: East African Little Collared Fruit Bat
children.description: A poorly known collared fruit bat restricted to the coastal forests of Kenya and Tanzania and the Albertine Rift forests, representing isolated forest relict populations. Listed as vulnerable due to its fragmented, declining forest habitat. Very small body mass (around 35 g); ecology essentially unstudied.

children.name: Myonycteris brachycephala
children.description: Endemic to the island of São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea; one of the island's few native mammals. A small fruit bat with a broad, short skull relative to the other collared bats. Vulnerable; restricted to a single small island with rapidly declining endemic forest.

children.name: Dobsonia
children.description: Large fruit bats unique for having their wing membranes fused along the backbone rather than attached to the sides of the body, giving the back a naked appearance. Found in the Philippines, Indonesia, New Guinea, and the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Dobsonia moluccensis
children.description: A large naked-backed fruit bat found from the Moluccas to New Guinea and northern Australia. Roosts in caves and mines in large colonies.

children.name: Dobsonia minor
children.description: A small Dobsonia of Sulawesi and the nearby islands. Its naked back and relatively large eyes distinguish it from other fruit bats sharing its range.

children.name: Dobsonia exolea
children.description: Endemic to Sulawesi, Indonesia. One of the most distinctive of the Dobsonia with its uniformly dark body and relatively long, pointed wings that give it a fast, direct flight unlike the more leisurely flapping of typical fruit bats — an adaptation that allows it to commute long distances across the fragmented forests of this geologically complex island.

children.name: Dobsonia anderseni
children.description: A species of the Bismarck Archipelago and the Solomon Islands. Named after the Danish mammalogist Knud Andersen. Little is known of its habits but it is believed to be primarily a cave-roosting species.
